user_goals_summary: Writes onboarding-oriented technical docs for competent engineers new to a specific system. Prioritizes building the reader's mental model over providing bare instructions — docs should explain not just what the system does but why alternatives were rejected. Structure should feel like a guided walkthrough, with warnings and context arriving before the reader needs them.

rubric.name: Decision Rationale
rubric.description: Architectural decisions include what was ruled out and why, not just what was chosen. The rejected alternatives and their tradeoffs are what teach the reader the mental model of the system.

dimensions.label: Each significant design choice names at least one rejected alternative
The reason for rejecting each alternative is stated in concrete terms (e.g., performance, complexity, blast radius)
The rationale appears close to where the decision is first introduced, not in a separate section
The cumulative effect of rationales gives the reader a coherent mental model of the system's priorities and constraints

rubric.name: Narrative Header Flow
rubric.description: Section headers read as a walkthrough, not an index. Each header implies what the reader is doing or experiencing at that point, creating a story arc through the document.

dimensions.label: Headers convey an action the reader is taking or an experience they're having, not just a topic label
Headers follow a logical sequence that mirrors the reader's actual workflow
Reading just the headers in order tells a coherent story of the onboarding path
No headers are bare noun phrases that merely name a concept (e.g., 'Token Refresh' should become something like 'What Happens When Your Token Expires')

rubric.name: Preventive Information Placement
rubric.description: Warnings, gotchas, and prerequisite knowledge appear before the section where the reader would first encounter the problem — not alongside or after the relevant instructions.

dimensions.label: Each warning or gotcha is positioned before the instructions it applies to, not embedded within or after them
Setup prerequisites (e.g., registration, configuration) appear before the first step that depends on them
Warnings are visually distinct from surrounding content (e.g., blockquotes, callout boxes, bold prefixes)
No critical failure-causing information is buried inside a paragraph the reader might skim

rubric.name: Audience Calibration
rubric.description: Assumes competence on general industry concepts while providing full depth on system-specific details. Does not over-explain what engineers already know, but doesn't leave gaps on what's unique to this system.

dimensions.label: Does not explain general concepts the target audience already knows (e.g., what a JWT is, how gRPC works)
System-specific details (configuration, endpoints, error codes, flows) are explained thoroughly
Uses domain jargon appropriate to the audience level without unnecessary definition
Provides enough context that the reader can act without asking the author for clarification

rubric.name: Self-Service Completeness
rubric.description: The document contains everything an engineer needs to get up and running independently — code snippets, error handling guidance, local dev setup, and reference links — so they don't need to ping the author.

dimensions.label: Includes copy-pasteable code snippets in the team's primary language(s) for key operations
Enumerates expected error states with what each means and what to do about it
Includes instructions or pointers for local development and testing
Links to related resources (proto files, runbooks, Slack channels, registries) so the reader can go deeper without asking

rubric.name: Empathy-Driven Troubleshooting
rubric.description: Common failure modes are framed from the engineer's experience — what they observe and feel — rather than from the system's perspective. Troubleshooting entries start with the symptom the reader would describe, then provide the explanation and fix.

dimensions.label: Each troubleshooting entry leads with the symptom the engineer would observe or complain about
Uses the reader's likely language (e.g., quoted frustrations) rather than system-centric descriptions
Each entry includes a concrete resolution or next step, not just an explanation of why it happens
Addresses the failure modes that would most commonly drive someone to ask the author for help

coaching_notes: This user's top priority is that docs build the reader's mental model — they'd rather spend extra sentences on 'why not X' than add more implementation detail. They think carefully about the reader's temporal experience through the document and will push back on information that arrives too late. Structure and information architecture matter more to them than prose style; content under headers was mostly fine from the first draft, but header framing and info placement required two revision rounds.

rubric.name: Preventive Information Placement
rubric.description: Warnings, gotchas, and prerequisite knowledge appear before the section where the reader would first encounter the problem — not alongside or after the relevant instructions.

dimensions.label: Each warning or gotcha is positioned before the instructions it applies to, not embedded within or after them
Setup prerequisites (e.g., registration, configuration) appear before the first step that depends on them
Warnings are visually distinct from surrounding content (e.g., blockquotes, callout boxes, bold prefixes)
No critical failure-causing information is buried inside a paragraph the reader might skim

rubric.name: Narrative Header Flow
rubric.description: Section headers at every level read as a walkthrough, not an index. Each header — including sub-headers within sections — implies what the reader is doing or experiencing at that point, creating a story arc through the document. A document with a single top-level header and no sub-headers fails this criterion regardless of how well the top-level header is worded.

dimensions.label: Headers convey an action the reader is taking or an experience they're having, not just a topic label
Headers follow a logical sequence that mirrors the reader's actual workflow
Reading just the headers in order tells a coherent story of the onboarding path
No headers are bare noun phrases that merely name a concept (e.g., 'Token Refresh' should become something like 'What Happens When Your Token Expires')
Documents longer than a short paragraph are divided into multiple headed sections — a single top-level title with no sub-headers does not satisfy this criterion

rubric.name: Empathy-Driven Troubleshooting
rubric.description: Common failure modes are framed from the engineer's experience — what they observe and feel — rather than from the system's perspective. Troubleshooting entries start with the symptom the reader would describe, then provide the explanation and fix.

dimensions.label: Each troubleshooting entry leads with the symptom the engineer would observe or complain about
Uses the reader's likely language (e.g., quoted frustrations) rather than system-centric descriptions
Each entry includes a concrete resolution or next step, not just an explanation of why it happens
Addresses the failure modes that would most commonly drive someone to ask the author for help

rubric.name: Proactive Contextual Teaching
rubric.description: The document anticipates where readers will import wrong mental models from prior experience or will wait passively for a failure signal instead of acting on available information. It inserts brief orienting notes at those moments — before the misapplication would occur — that reframe the reader's expectations without requiring them to fail first.

dimensions.label: Where this system behaves differently from common alternatives, the doc explicitly notes the difference (e.g., 'this differs from systems where...')
Where the reader could act on available information rather than waiting for an error, the doc shows them how (e.g., checking exp directly rather than waiting for a 401)
Orienting notes appear immediately alongside the behavior they contextualize, not in a separate background section

rubric.name: Audience Calibration
rubric.description: Assumes competence on general industry concepts while providing full depth on system-specific details. Does not over-explain what engineers already know, but doesn't leave gaps on what's unique to this system.

dimensions.label: Does not explain general concepts the target audience already knows (e.g., what a JWT is, how gRPC works)
System-specific details (configuration, endpoints, error codes, flows) are explained thoroughly
Uses domain jargon appropriate to the audience level without unnecessary definition
Provides enough context that the reader can act without asking the author for clarification

rubric.name: Self-Service Completeness
rubric.description: The document contains everything an engineer needs to get up and running independently — code snippets, error handling guidance, local dev setup, and reference links — so they don't need to ping the author. Code snippets must be minimal, self-contained, and immediately runnable in isolation; examples that require substantial surrounding scaffolding to execute do not satisfy this criterion.

dimensions.label: Includes copy-pasteable code snippets in the team's primary language(s) for key operations
Code snippets are minimal and self-contained — a reader can run or adapt them without needing to reconstruct missing context
Enumerates expected error states with what each means and what to do about it
Includes instructions or pointers for local development and testing
Links to related resources (proto files, runbooks, Slack channels, registries) so the reader can go deeper without asking

Structure should feel like a guided walkthrough, with warnings and context arriving before the reader needs them.

They think carefully about the reader's temporal experience through the document and will push back on information that arrives too late. Structure and information architecture matter more to them than prose style; content under headers was mostly fine from the first draft, but header framing and info placement required two revision rounds.

user_goals_summary: Writes onboarding-oriented docs for competent backend engineers new to a specific system. Prioritizes teaching the mental model behind architectural decisions (what was rejected and why) over just documenting what exists, and structures documents as guided walkthroughs where warnings always precede the mistakes they prevent.

rubric.name: Decision Rationale
rubric.description: Architectural decisions include what was ruled out and why, not just what was chosen. The rejected alternatives and their tradeoffs are what teach the reader the mental model of the system.

dimensions.label: Each significant design choice names at least one rejected alternative
The reason for rejecting each alternative is stated in concrete terms (e.g., performance, complexity, blast radius)
The rationale appears close to where the decision is first introduced, not in a separate section
The cumulative effect of rationales gives the reader a coherent mental model of the system's priorities and constraints

rubric.name: Preventive Information Placement
rubric.description: Warnings, gotchas, and prerequisite knowledge appear before the section where the reader would first encounter the problem — not alongside or after the relevant instructions.

dimensions.label: Each warning or gotcha is positioned before the instructions it applies to, not embedded within or after them
Setup prerequisites (e.g., registration, configuration) appear before the first step that depends on them
Warnings are visually distinct from surrounding content (e.g., blockquotes, callout boxes, bold prefixes)
No critical failure-causing information is buried inside a paragraph the reader might skim

rubric.description: Section headers at every level read as a walkthrough, not an index. Each header implies what the reader is doing or experiencing at that point, creating a story arc through the document.

dimensions.label: Headers convey an action the reader is taking or an experience they're having, not just a topic label
Headers follow a logical sequence that mirrors the reader's actual workflow
Reading just the headers in order tells a coherent story of the onboarding path
No headers are bare noun phrases that merely name a concept (e.g., 'Token Refresh' should become something like 'What Happens When Your Token Expires')
Documents longer than a short paragraph are divided into multiple headed sections — a single top-level title with no sub-headers does not satisfy this criterion

rubric.name: Empathy-Driven Troubleshooting
rubric.description: Common failure modes are framed from the engineer's experience — what they observe and feel — rather than from the system's perspective. Troubleshooting entries start with the symptom the reader would describe, then provide the explanation and fix.

dimensions.label: Each troubleshooting entry leads with the symptom the engineer would observe or complain about
Uses the reader's likely language (e.g., quoted frustrations) rather than system-centric descriptions
Each entry includes a concrete resolution or next step, not just an explanation of why it happens
Addresses the failure modes that would most commonly drive someone to ask the author for help

rubric.name: Proactive Contextual Teaching
rubric.description: The document anticipates where readers will import wrong mental models from prior experience or will wait passively for a failure signal instead of acting on available information. It inserts brief orienting notes at those moments — before the misapplication would occur — that reframe the reader's expectations without requiring them to fail first.

dimensions.label: Where this system behaves differently from common alternatives, the doc explicitly notes the difference (e.g., 'this differs from systems where...')
Where the reader could act on available information rather than waiting for an error, the doc shows them how (e.g., checking exp directly rather than waiting for a 401)
Orienting notes appear immediately alongside the behavior they contextualize, not in a separate background section

rubric.name: Audience Calibration
rubric.description: Assumes competence on general industry concepts while providing full depth on system-specific details. Does not over-explain what engineers already know, but doesn't leave gaps on what's unique to this system.

dimensions.label: Does not explain general concepts the target audience already knows (e.g., what a JWT is, how gRPC works)
System-specific details (configuration, endpoints, error codes, flows) are explained thoroughly
Uses domain jargon appropriate to the audience level without unnecessary definition
Provides enough context that the reader can act without asking the author for clarification

rubric.name: Self-Service Completeness
rubric.description: The document contains everything an engineer needs to get up and running independently — annotated code snippets, error handling guidance, local dev setup, and reference links — so they don't need to ping the author.

dimensions.label: Includes copy-pasteable code snippets that are minimal, self-contained, and can be run or adapted without reconstructing missing context
Each code snippet is preceded by a one-line comment describing what the snippet does (e.g., '// Decode and check expiration without making a network call')
Enumerates expected error states with what each means and what to do about it
Includes instructions or pointers for local development and testing
Links to related resources (proto files, runbooks, Slack channels, registries) so the reader can go deeper without asking

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every architectural decision must explain what was rejected and why — the user considers docs without rationale to be actively harmful to onboarding. Preferred trade-off: the user will accept a plausible rationale that can be refined later over a missing one; having *something* is better than a placeholder. Feedback pattern: the user consistently ranks their feedback by importance ('the bigger one' vs. 'the smaller thing') and acknowledges what's working before critiquing — mirror this structure when presenting revisions.

rubric.name: Precise Technical Tone
rubric.description: Uses exact, mechanistic descriptions of system behavior rather than softening or hedging language. Avoids words like 'simply,' 'just,' or 'easy' that minimize complexity or make readers feel they missed something obvious.

dimensions.label: Avoids words like 'simply,' 'just,' 'easy,' 'obviously' that minimize complexity or imply the reader should find something trivial
Describes system behavior in precise mechanical terms — what happens step by step — rather than vague summaries
Uses direct, imperative phrasing for instructions rather than hedging or passive constructions
Maintains a professional-direct register throughout — not overly formal, not casually chatty

coaching_notes: Decision rationale is this user's top non-negotiable — they will consistently flag any architectural statement that lacks a 'what we rejected and why.' Warning placement is their second priority: they read docs as a sequential workflow and expect the reader to be protected from mistakes before encountering the code that could cause them. They prefer to ship with clearly marked placeholders rather than fabricate details, but will eventually accept reasonable approximations if prompted. Style-wise, they are attuned to minimizing language ('simply') and treat it as a respect issue toward the reader.

user_goals_summary: Writes onboarding-oriented docs for competent backend engineers who are new to a specific system. Prioritizes teaching the mental model behind architectural decisions (not just describing what the system does), placing warnings before the code they protect, and creating a narrative walkthrough rather than a reference index. Values precision and directness over conversational softening.
coaching_notes: Decision rationale is this user's top non-negotiable — they will consistently flag any architectural statement that lacks a 'what we rejected and why.' Warning placement is their second priority: they read docs as a sequential workflow and expect the reader to be protected from mistakes before encountering the code that could cause them. They prefer to ship with clearly marked placeholders rather than fabricate details, but will eventually accept reasonable approximations if prompted. Style-wise, they are attuned to minimizing language ('simply') and treat it as a respect issue toward the reader.

Avoids words like 'simply,' 'just,' or 'easy' that minimize complexity or make readers feel they missed something obvious. Prose near code explains the reasoning behind specific values and thresholds, not merely restates what the code does.

dimensions.label: Avoids words like 'simply,' 'just,' 'easy,' 'obviously' that minimize complexity or imply the reader should find something trivial
Describes system behavior in precise mechanical terms — what happens step by step — rather than vague summaries
Uses direct, imperative phrasing for instructions rather than hedging or passive constructions
Maintains a professional-direct register throughout — not overly formal, not casually chatty
Prose near code explains why specific values or thresholds were chosen (e.g., why 60 seconds and not 30 or 120, why 5s timeout), not just what the code does

rubric.name: Narrative Header Flow
rubric.description: Section headers read as a walkthrough, not an index. Each header implies what the reader is doing or experiencing at that point, creating a story arc that an engineer following top-to-bottom feels guided by rather than lost in.

dimensions.label: Headers convey an action the reader is taking or an experience they're having, not just a topic label
Headers follow a logical sequence that mirrors the reader's actual workflow
Reading just the headers in order tells a coherent story of the onboarding path
No headers are bare noun phrases that merely name a concept (e.g., 'Expected error states' should become something like 'What to do when revocation calls go wrong')
Documents longer than a short paragraph are divided into multiple headed sections — a single top-level title with no sub-headers does not satisfy this criterion

rubric.name: Self-Service Completeness
rubric.description: The document contains everything an engineer needs to get up and running independently — annotated code snippets, error handling guidance, local dev setup, and reference links — so they don't need to ping the author on Slack.

dimensions.label: Every code snippet has a one-line comment above the block describing what it accomplishes at a glance — not a docstring inside, but a comment that orients the reader before they read the code
Code examples confirm what the surrounding prose already explains rather than re-explaining the same logic; long examples signal the prose left something unfinished
Code examples compile and run as written — no dead assignments, unused variables, missing imports, syntax errors, or patterns that would fail if copy-pasted into a file
Enumerates expected error states with what each means and what action to take
Includes local dev setup instructions and links to related resources (proto files, runbooks, Slack channels, registries) so the reader can go deeper without asking

rubric.name: Audience Calibration
rubric.description: Assumes competence on general industry concepts while providing full depth on system-specific details. Does not over-explain what engineers already know (e.g., what a JWT is), but leaves no gaps on what's unique to this system.

dimensions.label: Does not explain general concepts the target audience already knows (e.g., what a JWT is, how gRPC works)
System-specific details (configuration, endpoints, error codes, flows) are explained thoroughly
Uses domain jargon appropriate to the audience level without unnecessary definition
Provides enough context that the reader can act without asking the author for clarification

user_goals_summary: Produce onboarding-oriented internal docs that teach engineers the mental model of the system — not just what to do, but why the system works this way and what was considered and rejected. Docs should be self-contained enough that an engineer can get up and running without pinging the author, with warnings and gotchas placed before the reader can make the mistake.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiables in order of intensity: (1) rejected-alternative rationales — the user will explicitly call out missing 'why not' explanations and considers them the most valuable part of the doc; (2) warning placement before the relevant action — raised across every iteration; (3) one-line comment above every code block — raised three separate times, treat as a hard rule. The user's preferred trade-off is depth of reasoning over brevity — they'd rather a section be longer because it explains a tradeoff than shorter because it omits one. Code correctness is treated as a bug, not a style issue — a non-compiling snippet is equivalent to a factual error.
